1	# Artifact Generalization & Three-Layer Run Architecture
2	
3	Date: 2026-04-29
4	Status: Approved (refined through 3 review rounds with codex + fresh-eyes)
5	
6	## Summary
7	
8	Two threads converge in this plan:
9	
10	1. **Generalize artifacts beyond markdown documents.** Diagrams, wireframes, hero images, prompts — text and binary — become first-class artifacts via media-type plurality and sidecar `.ddx.yaml` files. Generated artifacts are checked into git; regeneration is source-hash-driven.
11	2. **Three-layer run architecture.** DDx owns three explicit layered primitives: `ddx run` (single agent invocation), `ddx try <bead>` (bead attempt in isolated worktree), `ddx work` (queue drain). The current two-class storage split (`.ddx/exec-runs/` vs `.ddx/executions/<attempt-id>/`) collapses into one substrate.
12	
13	These threads converge: **generating an artifact is a layer-1 agent run** with `produces_artifact` metadata.
14	
15	## Decisions (resolved through review)
16	
17	- **Artifact authority:** identity present → artifact. No "managed asset" second-class concept.
18	- **FEAT-019 fate:** child of FEAT-010 (not peer). Contracts to evaluation UX; workflow shapes (comparison, replay, benchmark) move to skills library.
19	- **Server posture:** 100% read coverage on HTTP/MCP for all DDx state, sequenced by impact. Writes case-by-case as workflows demand. Only `artifactRegenerate` write surface added in this plan.
20	- **Generated artifacts:** committed to git unconditionally. Size guard (50MB warn / 100MB hard error) with actionable git-lfs guidance; no auto-init / auto-`.gitattributes`.
21	- **CONTRACT-003:** amendments allowed if FEAT-006 work surfaces gaps. The mountable Cobra root export (below) is a load-bearing audit item.
22	- **Run substrate:** single on-disk shape; layer-2 and layer-3 add metadata. `.ddx/exec-runs/` and `.ddx/executions/<attempt-id>/` collapse.
23	- **No run-type catalog beyond the three layers.** Comparison, replay, benchmark, etc. are skill compositions, never enshrined in Go core or specs.
24	- **Loop ownership sharpened:** DDx owns mechanical queue drain (`ddx work`); content-aware supervisory decisions (e.g., "comparison failed → enqueue reconciliation beads") remain plugin/HELIX territory.
25	- **CLI verbs:** `ddx run` / `ddx try` / `ddx work` promoted to top-level. Hard-deprecate `ddx agent run`, `ddx agent execute-bead`, `ddx agent execute-loop` (exit non-zero with bare redirect message — no aliases, no grace period).
26	- **Generate-artifact layer placement:** pure-return generators (agent returns bytes/text, DDx writes file) are layer-1. Repo-editing generators are layer-2 with bead-less worktree.
27	- **Quorum dispatch:** moves to skill composition. `--quorum` flag goes away; replaced by `compare-prompts` skill.
28	- **`ddx agent` passthrough is structural:** DDx imports the upstream agent module's Cobra root and mounts it under `ddx agent`. Notionally `cli(ddx).agent(load_cli(agent))`. DDx does not enumerate per-subcommand wrappers; the entire upstream tree is the source of truth.
29	
30	## Three-layer architecture
31	
32	| Layer | CLI | Inputs | Outputs / side effects | Owns |
33	|---|---|---|---|---|
34	| 1 | `ddx run` | prompt + agent config | structured output, side effects, run metadata (tokens, model, duration, exit) | invocation atom; consumes upstream `ddx-agent` per CONTRACT-003 |
35	| 2 | `ddx try <bead>` | bead id, base revision | new worktree state + agent-run evidence; merge or preserve | worktree start/end capture; bead → prompt resolution; side-effect bundling |
36	| 3 | `ddx work` | bead queue, stop conditions | sequence of `ddx try` records + loop-level record (drained / blocked / deferred) | mechanical queue drain; no-progress detection |
37	
38	`ddx try` wraps `ddx run`. `ddx work` iterates `ddx try`. One on-disk substrate; layer metadata distinguishes records.
39	
40	`ddx artifact regenerate <id>` is sugar over layer 1 (or layer 2 if the generator edits the repo) with `produces_artifact: <id>` metadata.
41	
42	## Frame artifact updates
43	
44	### docs/helix/00-discover/product-vision.md
45	- Thesis: "documents AI agents consume" → "artifacts agents produce and consume."
46	- Artifact-management bullet: non-document media + generators.
47	- One sentence on producer/consumer space (no separate matrix).
48	- New Design-Philosophy subsection: **Three-layer run architecture** — `run` / `try` / `work` named as DDx's loop ownership.
49	- KVPs: "Multi-media artifact graph"; "Three-layer run architecture (run/try/work)"; note invocation is upstream.
50	- Principle #1: "Artifacts are the product (documents primary)."
51	
52	### docs/helix/01-frame/prd.md
53	- Summary: artifacts (multi-media); three-layer run architecture; one substrate.
54	- Problem: add "no provenance for generated artifacts."
55	- Goals: reword #1; add three-layer-architecture goal; add generated-artifact-regeneration goal; add "100% read coverage on HTTP/MCP."
56	- Non-goals: sharpen loop wording (mechanical drain in scope; supervisory content-aware decisions out). Add: "DDx does not catalog run types beyond the three layers."
57	- FEAT-001/005/006/007/008/010/019/021 blurbs updated.
58	
59	### FEAT-001-cli.md
60	- Promote `run` / `try` / `work` to top-level.
61	- `ddx agent` mounts upstream Cobra root structurally; no DDx-defined leaf subcommands beneath it.
62	- Hard-deprecation handlers for `ddx agent {run, execute-bead, execute-loop}`.
63	- New namespaces: `ddx runs` (cross-layer evidence introspection), `ddx tries` (layer-2 specifically), `ddx work workers` (layer-3 worker management).

107	## `ddx agent` subcommand fates
108	
109	`ddx agent` becomes a structural mount of the upstream agent CLI Cobra root. Per-file fate in DDx:
110	
111	**Delete with prejudice (upstream owns these via mounted subtree):**
112	- `agent_list.go`

131	**DDx-side overrides on the mounted subtree:**
132	- Deprecation handlers for `agent run` / `agent execute-bead` / `agent execute-loop`.
133	- `agent condense` (DDx-specific output filter — keep as override or move upstream during impl pass).

137	## Skill library (DDx-shipped workflows)
138	
139	Per FEAT-011's path model: source at `skills/ddx/`, embedded at `cli/internal/skills/ddx/`, init-time copies to `.claude/skills/ddx/`, `.agents/skills/ddx/`, `.ddx/skills/ddx/`.

1	---
2	name: ddx
3	description: Operates the DDx toolkit for document-driven development. Covers beads (work items), the queue, executions, agents, harnesses, personas, reviews, spec-id. Use when the user says "do work", "drain the queue", "run the next bead", "execute a bead", "review this", "check against spec", "what's on the queue", "what's ready", "create a bead", "file this as work", "run an agent", "dispatch", "use a persona", "how am I doing", "ddx doctor", or mentions any ddx CLI command.
4	---
5	
6	# DDx
7	
8	DDx (Document-Driven Development eXperience) is a CLI platform for
9	document-driven development. It ships a bead tracker (portable work
10	items with acceptance criteria), an agent service (harness dispatch
11	with profile-first routing), a persona system (bindable AI
12	personalities), a library registry (plugins with prompts, templates,
13	personas), and git-aware synchronization. This skill makes any
14	skills-compatible coding agent (Claude Code, OpenAI Codex, Gemini
15	CLI, etc.) understand and operate the DDx surface correctly.
16	
17	## How this skill works
18	
19	The skill body you're reading is an **overview** plus an **intent
20	router**. The real domain guidance lives in `reference/*.md` files.
21	
22	**Directive: before responding to any DDx-related request, read the
23	matching reference file from the router table below. The router is
24	not optional — your answer must be grounded in the reference file's
25	guidance, not this overview alone.**
26	
27	## Vocabulary
28	
29	Single source of truth for DDx terms. Every reference file uses these
30	exact definitions.
31	
32	- **Bead** — a portable work item (task, bug, epic, chore) with
33	  metadata, dependencies, and acceptance criteria. `ddx bead create`.
34	- **Queue** — the set of open beads. The *ready queue* is the subset
35	  with all dependencies closed. `ddx bead ready`, `ddx bead blocked`.
36	- **Ready** — a bead whose dependencies are all closed and which is
37	  eligible to be picked up next. `ddx bead ready`.
38	- **Blocked** — a bead with at least one unclosed dependency.
39	  `ddx bead blocked`.
40	- **Claim** — mark a bead as in-progress by an agent (concurrent-write
41	  protection). `ddx bead update <id> --claim`.
42	- **Close** — mark a bead as done, with evidence (session, commit
43	  SHA). `ddx bead close <id>`. Beads only close on execution outcomes
44	  `success` or `already_satisfied`.
45	- **Work** — drain the bead queue. `ddx work` (alias for
46	  `ddx agent execute-loop`); see `reference/work.md`.
47	- **Execute-bead** — the primitive: run one agent on one bead in an
48	  isolated worktree. `ddx agent execute-bead <id>`. The loop wraps
49	  this.
50	- **Execute-loop** — the queue-drain orchestrator; picks ready beads
51	  one at a time, calls execute-bead, closes on success, unclaims on
52	  failure. `ddx agent execute-loop` (or `ddx work`).
53	- **Execution** — a generic DDx execution run (FEAT-010); broader
54	  than execute-bead. Includes execution definitions, execution
55	  records, and execution evidence under `.ddx/executions/<id>/`.
56	- **Agent** — an AI coding agent (Claude, Codex, Gemini, etc.)
57	  invoked via a harness. Not a subagent (harness-specific — see
58	  below).
59	- **Harness** — the abstraction DDx uses to dispatch an agent
60	  (subprocess for claude/codex/gemini; embedded for the built-in
61	  `agent` harness). `ddx agent list`, `ddx agent capabilities`.
62	- **Persona** — a Markdown file (YAML frontmatter + body) that
63	  defines an AI personality. DDx injects the body as a system-prompt
64	  addendum to `ddx agent run`. `ddx persona list/show/bind`.
65	- **Role** — an abstract function (e.g., `code-reviewer`,
66	  `test-engineer`) a workflow can reference. Projects bind roles to
67	  personas.
68	- **Binding** — a project-specific `role: persona` map in
69	  `.ddx/config.yaml` under `persona_bindings`.
70	- **Profile** — intent-based routing policy for `ddx agent run` and
71	  `ddx work`: `default` (local-first with cloud escalation), `cheap`
72	  (local-only), `fast` (cloud-fast), `smart` (high-quality cloud).
73	  `--profile default|cheap|fast|smart`.
74	- **Plugin** — a self-contained extension installed to
75	  `.ddx/plugins/<name>/`. The default `ddx` plugin (personas,
76	  prompts, patterns, templates) is auto-installed by `ddx init`.
77	  `ddx install <name>`.
78	- **Skill** — an agentskills.io-standard directory (SKILL.md +
79	  optional `reference/`, `evals/`, `scripts/`). This `ddx` skill is
80	  the one DDx ships. Plugins can ship additional skills.
81	- **Subagent** — a harness-local concept for running a prompt in an
82	  isolated context (Claude Code's `.claude/agents/` + `context:
83	  fork`; Codex's `agents/`; others differ). DDx's `ddx agent run`
84	  (especially with `--quorum`) is the portable primitive; how a
85	  harness wraps that in a subagent is harness business. This skill
86	  does not take a position on subagent orchestration.
87	- **Update** — refresh plugin/toolkit content to a newer version.
88	  `ddx update [<plugin>]`.
89	- **Upgrade** — replace the DDx binary with a newer release.
90	  `ddx upgrade`.
91	- **Review** — two distinct concepts. **Bead review**
92	  (`ddx bead review <id>`) grades a completed bead against its
93	  acceptance criteria. **Quorum review** (`ddx agent run
94	  --quorum=<policy> --harnesses=<list>`) dispatches a review prompt
95	  across multiple harnesses and aggregates. See `reference/review.md`.
96	- **Governing artifact** — the document that authorizes a bead's
97	  work: a FEAT-\*, SD-\*, TD-\*, or ADR-\* under `docs/`. Referenced
98	  via `spec-id`.
99	- **Spec-id** — the `spec-id: <ID>` custom field on a bead pointing
100	  at its governing artifact.
101	
102	## Intent router
103	
104	Before responding, read the matching file.
105	
106	| User says / asks about | Read this file |
107	|---|---|
108	| write/plan work, "create a bead", "file this as work", bead metadata, acceptance criteria, dependencies | `reference/beads.md` |
109	| "do work", "drain the queue", "run the next bead", "execute a bead", "run work", verify-and-close | `reference/work.md` |
110	| "review this", "check against spec", bead review, quorum review, code review, adversarial check | `reference/review.md` |
111	| "run an agent", "dispatch", harnesses, profiles, models, effort, "use a persona", role bindings | `reference/agents.md` |
112	| "what's on the queue", "what's ready", "how am I doing", health check, "ddx doctor", sync status | `reference/status.md` |
113	
114	If the intent spans multiple files (e.g., "create a bead and then
115	run it"), read beads.md first, then work.md. If no match, ask the
116	user which concept they mean rather than guessing.
117	
118	## Top-level policy reminders
119	
120	These apply across all DDx operations. Do not restate them in every
121	reference file; do not violate them.
122	
123	- **Never edit `.ddx/beads.jsonl` directly.** All tracker changes go
124	  through `ddx bead create/update/close/dep`. Direct edits corrupt
125	  bead history and cannot be audited.
126	- **Tracker changes are commit-worthy.** After `ddx bead create`,
127	  `update`, `dep add/remove`, or `close`, commit the resulting
128	  `.ddx/beads.jsonl` change — either as a tracker-only commit or
129	  folded into the same commit as related implementation changes.
130	- **Preserve execute-bead commit history.** Branches containing
131	  `ddx agent execute-bead` commits carry an audit trail. **Never
132	  squash, rebase, filter, or amend** these commits. Use only
133	  `git merge --ff-only` or `git merge --no-ff` when merging.
134	  `gh pr merge --squash` and `--rebase` are forbidden on these
135	  branches.
136	- **Work in worktrees for parallel agents.** Use `wt switch -c
137	  <branch>` (worktrunk) or equivalent to give each concurrent agent
138	  its own isolated checkout. Execute-bead does this automatically;
139	  manual parallel work should too.
140	- **Profile-first agent dispatch.** Default to
141	  `ddx agent run --profile smart` (or `cheap`/`fast` for specific
142	  intents). Only override with `--harness`/`--model`/`--effort`
143	  when pinning for a controlled test or known provider bug.
144	
145	## Links out
146	
147	- Full CLI reference: `ddx --help`, `ddx <subcommand> --help`.
148	- Governing feature specs: see `FEAT-*` documents under your
149	  project's `docs/` tree — especially the CLI, beads, agent-service,
150	  executions, and skills features.
151	- Personas README: shipped by the default `ddx` plugin at
152	  `.ddx/plugins/ddx/personas/README.md`.
153	- Open standard this skill conforms to:
154	  [agentskills.io](https://agentskills.io).
155

1	# Review — Bead Review and Quorum Review
2	
3	DDx has two distinct review concepts. They are **not interchangeable**.
4	
5	| Concept | Command | What it does |
6	|---|---|---|
7	| **Bead review** | `ddx bead review <id>` | Grades a bead's implementation against its declared acceptance criteria. Per-AC verdict with evidence. |
8	| **Quorum review** | `ddx agent run --quorum=<policy> --harnesses=<list>` | Dispatches a review prompt across multiple harnesses and aggregates the verdicts. Used for high-stakes or adversarial checks. |
9	
10	## Bead review
11	
12	```bash
13	ddx bead review <bead-id>
14	```
15	
16	Generates a review-ready prompt that includes:
17	
18	- Bead title, description, acceptance criteria (verbatim).
19	- Governing artifact content (follows `spec-id`).
20	- The diff since the bead's base commit (execution evidence commits
21	  excluded — see the related bug ddx-39e27896 for exclusion
22	  pathspec).
23	- Review instructions: output contract, verdict taxonomy.
24	
25	The reviewer (whoever receives the prompt — a human, a harness via
26	`ddx agent run`, or a quorum) produces a structured verdict:
27	
28	```
29	## Bead review: ddx-<id>
30	
31	### Verdict: APPROVE | REQUEST_CHANGES | BLOCK
32	
33	### Per-criterion
34	
35	1. [AC text]
36	   Verdict: APPROVE | REQUEST_CHANGES | BLOCK
37	   Evidence: <file:line, test output, diff hunk, or explicit "not verifiable">
38	
39	2. [AC text]
40	   ...
41	
42	### Summary
43	<1-3 sentences of overall context>
44	
45	### Findings
46	<detailed issues, if any; omit when verdict is APPROVE with no findings>
47	```
48	
49	The review output can be stored as bead evidence:
50	
51	```bash
52	ddx bead evidence add <id> --type review --body <path-to-review.md>
53	```
54	
55	## Quorum review
56	
57	```bash
58	ddx agent run --quorum=majority --harnesses=claude,codex,gemini \
59	  --prompt review-prompt.md
60	```
61	
62	`--quorum=<policy>` values:
63	
64	- `majority` — at least ⌈N/2⌉ harnesses must agree on the verdict.
65	- `unanimous` — all harnesses must agree.
66	- `any` — pass if any harness APPROVES (rarely useful; adversarial
67	  checks should require stronger consensus).
68	
69	Use quorum when:
70	
71	- The change is high-stakes (migrations, security surface, API
72	  contracts, auth).
73	- The review surface is controversial and one harness's opinion
74	  isn't enough.
75	- You want adversarial pressure against a single-model blindspot
76	  (different model families have different failure modes).
77	
78	The aggregated output shows each harness's verdict side-by-side plus
79	the aggregate decision. Disagreements are surfaced, not hidden.
80	
81	## Required evidence format
82	
83	Both review kinds share an evidence contract: **no verdict without
84	evidence.**
85	
86	- "APPROVE" with no `Evidence:` lines → reject as malformed.
87	- "REQUEST_CHANGES" without pointing at the change needed → reject.
88	- "BLOCK" without a reason → reject (see the related bug
89	  ddx-39e27896 about `review-malfunction` retries).
90	
91	Evidence takes one of these forms:
92	
93	1. **File:line reference**: `cli/internal/bead/store.go:142`
94	2. **Test output**: `FAIL: TestBeadClose_UnclaimsOnNonSuccess (0.01s)`
95	3. **Diff hunk**: a short quote from the diff showing the offending
96	   or approved change.
97	4. **Explicit "not verifiable"**: the AC is written in a way that
98	   cannot be mechanically checked; flag this as a bead-authoring
99	   issue rather than silently approving.
100	
101	## Anti-patterns
102	
103	- **Hallucinated approval.** "Looks good to me!" with no per-AC
104	  grading. The review is worthless.
105	- **Reviewing the prompt instead of the diff.** The reviewer evaluated
106	  "what you intended" not "what you actually changed." Verdict must
107	  reference the diff or the resulting code.
108	- **Verdict without evidence.** Every verdict must have at least one
109	  evidence line; APPROVE is no exception (evidence can be "AC met:
110	  `go test ./foo/... passes` output attached").
111	- **Silencing disagreement in quorum.** If two harnesses disagree,
112	  don't collapse to a single "decision." Surface the disagreement
113	  in the output and let the human decide.
114	- **Quorum for routine work.** Single-harness bead review is fine
115	  for most beads. Reserve quorum for the cases that justify the
116	  cost.
117	
118	## Subagents: not this skill's business
119	
120	Some harnesses can run a quorum in forked/isolated subagent
121	contexts; each harness implements this differently. This skill
122	doesn't specify how isolation is achieved —
123	`ddx agent run --quorum=<policy> --harnesses=<list>` is the
124	portable invocation, and the harness decides whether to fork,
125	spawn, or run inline.
126	
127	## CLI reference
128	
129	```bash
130	# Bead review (single-reviewer AC grading)
131	ddx bead review <id>                           # generate review prompt
132	ddx bead review <id> --execute --harness claude  # dispatch and grade
133	ddx bead evidence add <id> --type review --body r.md  # store review
134	
135	# Quorum review (multi-harness)
136	ddx agent run --quorum=majority \
137	  --harnesses=claude,codex \
138	  --prompt review.md
139	
140	ddx agent run --quorum=unanimous \
141	  --harnesses=claude,codex,gemini \
142	  --prompt review.md
143	```
144	
145	Full flag list: `ddx bead review --help`, `ddx agent run --help`.
146

1	# Agents — Harness, Profile, Persona Dispatch
2	
3	> **Debugging routing?** Routing lives entirely in ddx-agent. See `CONTRACT-003-ddx-agent-service` (in the agent repo, `docs/helix/02-design/contracts/`) for the public surface; routing internals are inside that module.
4	
5	`ddx agent run` is the unified interface for invoking an AI coding
6	agent through any of DDx's harnesses. Routing decisions (which
7	harness, which model, which effort level) should flow from
8	**intent**, not from hand-picking implementation details.
9	
10	## Profile-first routing
11	
12	Default to profile:
13	
14	```bash
15	ddx agent run --profile default --prompt task.md
16	```
17	
18	Each profile names a **tier escalation ladder**. Routing tries the first
19	tier; if it fails with an escalation-triggering outcome, it retries at
20	the next tier. The four shipped profiles:
21	
22	- `cheap` — **local only**. Ladder: `[cheap]`. Never escalates. High-
23	  volume exploratory work where cost dominates and adequate quality
24	  suffices.
25	- `default` — **local first, escalate on failure**. Ladder:
26	  `[cheap, standard, smart]`. The common case. Tries the local model
27	  first; escalates to cloud only when the local path genuinely fails
28	  (execution_failed, land_conflict, post_run_check_failed,
29	  structural_validation_failed). Throughput-per-dollar optimized.
30	- `fast` — **cloud-fast, skip local**. Ladder: `[fast, smart]`. For
31	  latency-critical or interactive work where the local model's 9s
32	  reasoning warmup is unacceptable. Escalates fast→smart on failure.
33	- `smart` — **cloud high-quality, no escalation**. Ladder: `[smart]`.
34	  For bead review, adversarial checks, beads whose AC demands high-
35	  quality reasoning. If smart fails, the work fails meaningfully — no
36	  silent fallback.
37	
38	Profiles are the portable intent signal. DDx resolves each ladder tier's
39	model ref to a concrete (harness, model, effort) tuple per the project's
40	`.ddx/config.yaml` `routing.profile_ladders` + `model_overrides` and the
41	shared catalog. See FEAT-006 §"Profile Semantics" for the full contract.
42	
43	## Explicit overrides
44	
45	Override routing only when you have a reason:
46	
47	```bash
48	ddx agent run --harness codex --prompt task.md    # force a harness
49	ddx agent run --model qwen3.6 --prompt task.md    # exact model pin
50	ddx agent run --effort high --prompt task.md      # effort override
51	```
52	
53	Don't stack `--profile` and `--model` together unintentionally —
54	`--model` is an override that supersedes the profile. If you want
55	"smart profile but pinned to this model", that's the model; drop
56	the profile flag.
57	
58	**Reasons to override:**
59	
60	- Reproducing a bug specific to one harness or model.
61	- Controlled A/B tests between harnesses.
62	- Provider-specific features (e.g., Claude extended thinking).
63	- Cost/quota management on a specific provider.
64	
65	**Reasons NOT to override:**
66	
67	- "This harness is my favorite." Use the profile and let routing
68	  decide.
69	- "I don't trust the routing." File a bug; don't work around it.
70	
71	## Personas
72	
73	Personas are reusable AI personality templates. DDx injects a
74	persona's body as a system-prompt addendum to `ddx agent run` —
75	same persona, same behavior across every harness, because the
76	harness sees a flat system prompt, not a persona file.
77	
78	### Default roster
79	
80	The default `ddx` plugin ships five role-focused personas:
81	
82	- `code-reviewer` — security-first review with structured verdicts
83	- `test-engineer` — stubs-over-mocks, real-e2e, baselined
84	  performance, testing pyramid as shape not ratios
85	- `implementer` — YAGNI / KISS / DOWITYTD, ships tests with code
86	- `architect` — opinionated on when to reach for each pattern
87	  (monolith-first, data-model-first)
88	- `specification-enforcer` — refuses drift from governing artifacts
89	
90	See `.ddx/plugins/ddx/personas/README.md` for the quality bar and
91	authoring guidance. Projects can install additional personas via
92	plugins.
93	
94	### Using a persona
95	
96	Directly at invocation time:
97	
98	```bash
99	ddx agent run --persona code-reviewer --prompt review.md
100	```
101	
102	Or by binding a role in `.ddx/config.yaml`:
103	
104	```yaml
105	persona_bindings:
106	  code-reviewer: code-reviewer
107	  test-engineer: test-engineer
108	  architect: architect
109	```
110	
111	Workflows then reference the role abstractly (e.g., "dispatch to
112	the `code-reviewer` role"), and DDx resolves the binding at
113	dispatch time.
114	
115	```bash
116	ddx persona list                # available personas
117	ddx persona show <name>         # persona body + frontmatter
118	ddx persona bind <role> <name>  # set a binding
119	ddx persona bindings            # current role → persona map
120	```
121	
122	## Quorum
123	
124	For multi-harness review or adversarial checks, use quorum dispatch
125	— covered in `reference/review.md`:
126	
127	```bash
128	ddx agent run --quorum=majority --harnesses=claude,codex \
129	  --prompt review.md
130	```
131	
132	## Getting more capabilities
133	
134	`ddx install <name>` adds plugins to the project. Plugins can ship
135	personas, prompts, patterns, templates, and workflow skills.
136	
137	```bash
138	ddx install helix              # HELIX workflow plugin
139	ddx search <term>              # discover available plugins
140	ddx installed                  # list installed plugins
141	ddx uninstall <name>           # remove
142	```
143	
144	Custom personas go in `.ddx/plugins/<plugin>/personas/<name>.md`
145	(or directly in `library/personas/<name>.md` for local-only use).
146	See the personas README for the authoring quality bar.
147	
148	## Anti-patterns
149	
150	- **Stacking `--profile` and `--model` / `--effort`**. If `--model`
151	  is set, `--profile` is moot. Pick one intent signal.
152	- **Hardcoding a harness name in a workflow**. Workflow should
153	  dispatch by profile or role binding; letting each project choose
154	  its harness is the point of DDx's routing layer.
155	- **Persona for everything**. Personas add a system-prompt addendum;
156	  they don't make a bad prompt good. Start with a clear prompt;
157	  reach for a persona when you want consistent style/standards
158	  across invocations.
159	- **Persona files in skill directories**. Personas live in
160	  `library/personas/` or `.ddx/plugins/<plugin>/personas/`, not in
161	  `.claude/skills/` or `.agents/skills/`. Don't mix the two.
162	
163	## CLI reference
164	
165	```bash
166	# Dispatch
167	ddx agent run --profile smart --prompt task.md
168	ddx agent run --harness claude --prompt task.md
169	ddx agent run --persona code-reviewer --prompt review.md
170	ddx agent run --quorum=majority --harnesses=claude,codex --prompt p.md
171	ddx agent run --automation=plan|auto|yolo
172	
173	# Introspection
174	ddx agent list                         # available harnesses
175	ddx agent capabilities <harness>       # model + effort options
176	ddx agent doctor                       # harness health
177	ddx agent log                          # recent invocation metadata
178	ddx agent log <session-id>             # one invocation's detail
179	
180	# Personas
181	ddx persona list
182	ddx persona show <name>
183	ddx persona bind <role> <persona>
184	ddx persona bindings
185	
186	# Plugin install
187	ddx install <plugin>
188	ddx search <term>
189	ddx installed
190	ddx uninstall <name>
191	```
192	
193	Full flag list: `ddx agent run --help`, `ddx persona --help`,
194	`ddx install --help`.
195

1	---
2	ddx:
3	  id: FEAT-011

5	    - helix.prd

9	---
10	# Feature: DDx Agent Skills
11	
12	**ID:** FEAT-011
13	**Status:** Revising (consolidation in progress; see Phase 1 epic in beads)
14	**Priority:** P1
15	**Owner:** DDx Team
16	
17	## Overview
18	
19	DDx ships a single agent-facing skill (`ddx`) that makes any
20	skills-compatible coding agent "ddx-aware" after `ddx init`. The skill
21	is written to the [agentskills.io](https://agentskills.io) open
22	standard so it works identically in Claude Code, OpenAI Codex, Gemini
23	CLI, Cursor, OpenCode, and any other harness that implements the
24	standard.
25	
26	When the user says "do work", "review this", "what's on the queue",
27	"create a bead", or any DDx concept, the harness discovers the `ddx`
28	skill via its description, loads `SKILL.md`, and routes via an
29	explicit intent table into `reference/*.md` files that contain the
30	domain guidance.
31	
32	## Problem Statement
33	
34	Prior iterations of FEAT-011 shipped ~7 sibling skills
35	(`ddx-bead`, `ddx-agent`, `ddx-run`, `ddx-review`, `ddx-status`,
36	`ddx-install`, `ddx-doctor`). Real-world usage exposed problems:
37	
38	- **Intent ambiguity.** Users say "do work", not "/ddx-run". A flat
39	  list of named slash commands forces the harness to guess between
40	  `/ddx-bead` vs `/ddx-run` vs `/ddx-work` from natural-language
41	  phrases.
42	- **Vocabulary drift.** Each skill redefined terms (bead, queue,
43	  harness, review) inline; wording diverged across files and from
44	  FEAT-* specs.
45	- **Workflow-opinion leakage.** `ddx-bead` mandated `helix` labels
46	  and documented `phase:*` labels — HELIX methodology opinions
47	  inside a core DDx skill.
48	- **Skill tree drift.** Two copies of most skills
49	  (`cli/internal/skills/` embedded vs top-level `/skills/`) with
50	  real content divergence.
51	- **Init gap.** `ddx init` copied only 2 of 7 embedded skills; the
52	  rest never surfaced to Claude Code unless the user re-installed
53	  manually.
54	- **Portability.** Skills used Claude-Code-only frontmatter fields
55	  (`argument-hint`) that are silently ignored by Codex and other
56	  harnesses, and reached for Claude-Code-only patterns
57	  (`context: fork`) that don't exist elsewhere.
58	
59	The consolidated design fixes each of these.
60	
61	## Architecture
62	
63	### Single skill, progressive disclosure
64	
65	```
66	skills/ddx/
67	├── SKILL.md                # overview, vocabulary, intent-router directive
68	├── reference/
69	│   ├── beads.md            # writing execution-ready beads (best practices)
70	│   ├── work.md             # draining the queue, execute-bead, verify + close
71	│   ├── review.md           # bead-review (AC grade) + quorum code review
72	│   ├── agents.md           # harness/profile dispatch, personas
73	│   └── status.md           # queue state, doctor, health checks
74	└── evals/
75	    └── routing.jsonl       # ~15 phrase→expected-routing fixtures
76	```
77	
78	At harness startup, only the skill's `name` + `description` metadata
79	is pre-loaded. On activation, the harness reads `SKILL.md`. When the
80	intent router matches a phrase, the harness reads the matching
81	`reference/*.md`. Nothing else loads. This is the Anthropic
82	"Pattern 2: Domain-specific organization" pattern and matches how
83	Codex and other agentskills.io implementations progressively disclose
84	skill content.
85	
86	### Portability contract
87	
88	Frontmatter is the portable-safe minimum — `name` + `description` only.
89	
90	```yaml
91	---
92	name: ddx
93	description: Operates the DDx toolkit for document-driven development. Covers beads (work items), the queue, executions, agents, harnesses, personas, reviews, spec-id. Use when the user says "do work", "drain the queue", "run the next bead", "execute a bead", "review this", "check against spec", "what's on the queue", "what's ready", "create a bead", "file this as work", "run an agent", "dispatch", "use a persona", "how am I doing", "ddx doctor", or mentions any ddx CLI command.
94	---
95	```
96	
97	No `argument-hint`, `when_to_use`, `context: fork`, `allowed-tools`,
98	`disable-model-invocation`, `user-invocable`, `paths`, `model`,
99	`effort`, `agent`, or `hooks` fields — those are Claude-Code
100	extensions that Codex and others ignore or reject. The description
101	front-loads the DDx nouns (bead, queue, execution, harness, persona,
102	spec-id) **and** the exact verb phrases users say verbatim, because
103	implicit-invocation matchers prefer substring-ish keyword matching
104	over semantic understanding.
105	
106	### Intent-router directive
107	
108	Claude does not reliably auto-chase reference links; the router in
109	`SKILL.md` is stated as an **explicit directive**, not a hint:
110	
111	> "Before responding to any DDx-related request, read the matching
112	> reference file below. The router is not optional — your answer must
113	> be grounded in the reference file's guidance, not this overview
114	> alone."
115	
116	### Subagents: ship none
117	
118	Subagent orchestration is harness-specific (Claude Code has
119	`.claude/agents/` + `context: fork`; Codex has its own subagent
120	surface; others differ). The `ddx` skill describes *actions*
121	("run `ddx agent run --quorum=<policy>`") and lets each harness
122	decide how to run them. Quorum review is a CLI invocation, not a
123	skill-frontmatter directive.
124	
125	### Installation
126	
127	> See FEAT-015 (2026-04-29 amendment) for the authoritative install
128	> model: project-local copy semantics, zero symlinks created by
129	> `ddx install`, and the no-`~` manifest invariant.
130	
131	- `ddx init` copies `skills/ddx/` into `.claude/skills/ddx/`,
132	  `.agents/skills/ddx/`, and `.ddx/skills/ddx/` as real files
133	  (symlinks break after `git clone` on a fresh machine, and DDx no
134	  longer creates symlinks during install — see FEAT-015).
135	- On init and on `ddx update`, stale ddx-prefixed skill directories
136	  from prior DDx versions are removed:
137	  `ddx-bead`, `ddx-run`, `ddx-agent`, `ddx-review`, `ddx-status`,
138	  `ddx-doctor`, `ddx-install`, `ddx-release`. Third-party skills are
139	  untouched.
140	- Skills embed into the binary via `//go:embed all:ddx` against a
141	  copy under `cli/internal/skills/ddx/`. Because `go:embed` cannot
142	  traverse upward, a `make copy-skills` target rsyncs
143	  `skills/ddx/` → `cli/internal/skills/ddx/` before every build.
144	
145	### AGENTS.md: merge, not clobber
146	
147	Codex treats `AGENTS.md` as primary guidance before work, and users
148	may have added content. `ddx init` uses marker-delimited injection:
149	
150	```markdown
151	<!-- DDX-AGENTS:START -->
152	This project uses DDx. Use the `ddx` skill for beads, work, review,
153	agents, and status.
154	
155	(tracker/merge policy follows)
156	<!-- DDX-AGENTS:END -->
157	```
158	
159	Content outside the markers is preserved. The block says
160	"the `ddx` skill", not "`/ddx`" (which is Claude-specific slash-
161	command phrasing). Re-running `ddx init` updates the block in place.
162	
163	### Evaluation-driven validation
164	
165	Anthropic's skill-authoring guidance treats evaluations as
166	load-bearing, not optional. The repo ships:
167	
168	- `skills/ddx/evals/routing.jsonl` — ~15 rows, each a user phrase +
169	  expected reference file + expected CLI invocation, covering every
170	  intent-router entry and edge phrasings.
171	- `scripts/eval-skill.sh` — driver that runs each row against
172	  `--harness claude` and `--harness codex` and verifies routing.
173	  `--validate` mode does agentskills.io spec conformance.
174	- `make eval-skill` in CI on PRs that touch `skills/ddx/`.
175	
176	## Requirements
177	
178	### Functional
179	
180	1. DDx ships exactly one skill (`ddx`) in `skills/ddx/`; no sibling
181	   `ddx-*` skill directories.
182	2. `SKILL.md` frontmatter contains only `name` and `description`.
183	3. `SKILL.md` body is under 500 lines and includes an explicit
184	   intent-router directive.
185	4. `reference/*.md` files are linked one level deep from `SKILL.md`.
186	5. `ddx init` copies the skill, removes stale `ddx-*` dirs, and
187	   merges the AGENTS.md block without clobbering user content.
188	6. `ddx update` refreshes `.claude/skills/ddx/` and removes stale
189	   dirs.
190	7. `skills/ddx/evals/routing.jsonl` contains at least 15 rows, each
191	   passing against `claude` and `codex` harnesses via
192	   `make eval-skill`.
193	8. `SKILL.md` passes `scripts/eval-skill.sh --validate` (agentskills.io
194	   spec conformance).
195	
196	### Non-Functional
197	
198	- Skills work with any agent supporting the agentskills.io standard;
199	  no Claude-Code-only frontmatter or directives.
200	- Skills are plain Markdown — no runtime dependencies.
201	- Skills degrade gracefully if DDx CLI is not installed (clear error).
202	- HELIX-specific rules (`helix` label requirement, `phase:*`
203	  enumeration) do not appear in the `ddx` skill; HELIX opinions ship
204	  in the HELIX plugin.
205	
206	## User Stories
207	
208	### US-110: Harness routes natural-language DDx intent
209	**As a** user in a DDx project using Claude Code, Codex, or any other
210	skills-compatible harness
211	**I want** phrases like "do work", "drain the queue", "review this",
212	"what's on the queue", "create a bead" to route to DDx guidance
213	**So that** I don't have to remember slash-command names
214	
215	**Acceptance Criteria:**
216	- Running `make eval-skill` passes all rows against both `--harness claude`
217	  and `--harness codex`.
218	- Each intent-router entry in `SKILL.md` has at least one matching
219	  row in `routing.jsonl`.
220	
221	### US-111: Skill stays under the token budget
222	**As a** harness loading the `ddx` skill
223	**I want** `SKILL.md` body under 500 lines
224	**So that** skill activation stays within the Anthropic-recommended
225	token budget and doesn't compete with conversation context
226	
227	**Acceptance Criteria:**
228	- `wc -l skills/ddx/SKILL.md` < 500.
229	- `scripts/eval-skill.sh --validate` passes.
230	
231	### US-112: `ddx init` handles existing projects cleanly
232	**As a** user upgrading from an older DDx version with
233	`.claude/skills/ddx-run/` and similar dirs already present
234	**I want** `ddx init` to remove the old dirs and install only the
235	new single-skill layout
236	**So that** the harness doesn't see stale, conflicting skills
237	
238	**Acceptance Criteria:**
239	- In a dir pre-seeded with
240	  `.claude/skills/{ddx-run,ddx-doctor,ddx-bead}/`, running
241	  `ddx init` leaves only `.claude/skills/ddx/`.
242	- Third-party skills under `.claude/skills/` are untouched.
243	
244	### US-113: `AGENTS.md` merge preserves user content
245	**As a** user who has added content to `AGENTS.md`
246	**I want** `ddx init` to inject the DDx block without clobbering
247	what I wrote
248	**So that** my Codex / Claude / Gemini setup isn't broken
249	
250	**Acceptance Criteria:**
251	- Given an `AGENTS.md` with user content both before and after the
252	  `<!-- DDX-AGENTS:START -->` / `<!-- DDX-AGENTS:END -->` markers,
253	  running `ddx init` updates the block between markers and preserves
254	  everything outside.
255	- Running `ddx init` a second time does not duplicate the block.
256	
257	### US-114: `ddx update` refreshes skills
258	**As a** user who ran `ddx init` on an older DDx version
259	**I want** `ddx update` to refresh `.claude/skills/ddx/` to the
260	current shipped content
261	**So that** I don't have to re-run `ddx init` to pick up skill
262	improvements
263	
264	**Acceptance Criteria:**
265	- After `ddx update`, `.claude/skills/ddx/` bytes match the embedded

17	## Overview
18	
19	DDx ships a single agent-facing skill (`ddx`) that makes any
20	skills-compatible coding agent "ddx-aware" after `ddx init`. The skill
21	is written to the [agentskills.io](https://agentskills.io) open
22	standard so it works identically in Claude Code, OpenAI Codex, Gemini
23	CLI, Cursor, OpenCode, and any other harness that implements the
24	standard.
25	
26	When the user says "do work", "review this", "what's on the queue",
27	"create a bead", or any DDx concept, the harness discovers the `ddx`
28	skill via its description, loads `SKILL.md`, and routes via an
29	explicit intent table into `reference/*.md` files that contain the
30	domain guidance.
31	
32	## Problem Statement
33	
34	Prior iterations of FEAT-011 shipped ~7 sibling skills
35	(`ddx-bead`, `ddx-agent`, `ddx-run`, `ddx-review`, `ddx-status`,
36	`ddx-install`, `ddx-doctor`). Real-world usage exposed problems:
37	
38	- **Intent ambiguity.** Users say "do work", not "/ddx-run". A flat
39	  list of named slash commands forces the harness to guess between
40	  `/ddx-bead` vs `/ddx-run` vs `/ddx-work` from natural-language
41	  phrases.
42	- **Vocabulary drift.** Each skill redefined terms (bead, queue,
43	  harness, review) inline; wording diverged across files and from
44	  FEAT-* specs.
45	- **Workflow-opinion leakage.** `ddx-bead` mandated `helix` labels
46	  and documented `phase:*` labels — HELIX methodology opinions
47	  inside a core DDx skill.
48	- **Skill tree drift.** Two copies of most skills
49	  (`cli/internal/skills/` embedded vs top-level `/skills/`) with
50	  real content divergence.
51	- **Init gap.** `ddx init` copied only 2 of 7 embedded skills; the
52	  rest never surfaced to Claude Code unless the user re-installed
53	  manually.
54	- **Portability.** Skills used Claude-Code-only frontmatter fields
55	  (`argument-hint`) that are silently ignored by Codex and other
56	  harnesses, and reached for Claude-Code-only patterns
57	  (`context: fork`) that don't exist elsewhere.
58	
59	The consolidated design fixes each of these.
60	
61	## Architecture
62	
63	### Single skill, progressive disclosure
64	
65	```
66	skills/ddx/
67	├── SKILL.md                # overview, vocabulary, intent-router directive
68	├── reference/
69	│   ├── beads.md            # writing execution-ready beads (best practices)
70	│   ├── work.md             # draining the queue, execute-bead, verify + close
71	│   ├── review.md           # bead-review (AC grade) + quorum code review
72	│   ├── agents.md           # harness/profile dispatch, personas
73	│   └── status.md           # queue state, doctor, health checks
74	└── evals/
75	    └── routing.jsonl       # ~15 phrase→expected-routing fixtures
76	```
77	
78	At harness startup, only the skill's `name` + `description` metadata
79	is pre-loaded. On activation, the harness reads `SKILL.md`. When the
80	intent router matches a phrase, the harness reads the matching
81	`reference/*.md`. Nothing else loads. This is the Anthropic
82	"Pattern 2: Domain-specific organization" pattern and matches how
83	Codex and other agentskills.io implementations progressively disclose
84	skill content.
85	
86	### Portability contract
87	
88	Frontmatter is the portable-safe minimum — `name` + `description` only.
89	
90	```yaml
91	---
92	name: ddx
93	description: Operates the DDx toolkit for document-driven development. Covers beads (work items), the queue, executions, agents, harnesses, personas, reviews, spec-id. Use when the user says "do work", "drain the queue", "run the next bead", "execute a bead", "review this", "check against spec", "what's on the queue", "what's ready", "create a bead", "file this as work", "run an agent", "dispatch", "use a persona", "how am I doing", "ddx doctor", or mentions any ddx CLI command.
94	---
95	```
96	
97	No `argument-hint`, `when_to_use`, `context: fork`, `allowed-tools`,
98	`disable-model-invocation`, `user-invocable`, `paths`, `model`,
99	`effort`, `agent`, or `hooks` fields — those are Claude-Code
100	extensions that Codex and others ignore or reject. The description
101	front-loads the DDx nouns (bead, queue, execution, harness, persona,
102	spec-id) **and** the exact verb phrases users say verbatim, because
103	implicit-invocation matchers prefer substring-ish keyword matching
104	over semantic understanding.
105	
106	### Intent-router directive
107	
108	Claude does not reliably auto-chase reference links; the router in
109	`SKILL.md` is stated as an **explicit directive**, not a hint:
110

112	> reference file below. The router is not optional — your answer must
113	> be grounded in the reference file's guidance, not this overview
114	> alone."

123	skill-frontmatter directive.

---
name: adversarial-review
description: Run an adversarial review of a plan, spec, bead, or PR by dispatching to multiple harnesses with adversarial framing and aggregating findings. Use when you want pressure-testing against a single-model blindspot, high-stakes review, or multi-round critique before committing to a direction.
---

# Adversarial Review

A workflow skill for pressure-testing a target (plan, spec, bead, code change,
or PR) by dispatching adversarial review prompts to multiple harnesses and
aggregating the findings. Replaces ad-hoc `--quorum` invocations with a
structured, repeatable workflow.

## When to use

- **Plans and specs** before breaking into beads — catch ambiguity and missing
  constraints early.
- **High-stakes beads** — migrations, auth, API contracts, security surface.
- **Controversial changes** — one model's opinion is not enough; different model
  families catch different failure modes.
- **Multi-round refinement** — run 2–3 rounds; use findings to revise; re-run
  until BLOCKING count drops to zero.

Single-harness bead review (`ddx bead review <id>`) is fine for routine work.
Reserve adversarial-review for the cases that justify the cost.

## Workflow

### 1. Prepare the target

Gather the content to review into a single prompt file. Include:

- The artifact being reviewed (plan text, spec section, bead description + AC,
  or diff / PR summary).
- The question you want pressure-tested ("Is this design complete?", "What are
  the failure modes?", "What does this spec leave undefined?").
- Any governing artifacts the reviewer should check against (FEAT-\* IDs,
  CONTRACT-\* IDs, ADR links).

Write the prompt to a file, e.g. `review-target.md`:

```markdown
## Target

<paste plan, spec excerpt, or diff>

## Governing artifacts

- FEAT-010: docs/helix/01-frame/features/FEAT-010-executions.md
- (add others)

## Review question

Find BLOCKING issues: ambiguities, missing constraints, design gaps,
internal contradictions, or anything that would cause implementation
rework. Be adversarial — your job is to find problems, not validate.
```

### 2. Dispatch to multiple harnesses

Run each harness in parallel using `ddx agent run --harness <name>`.
Use `--profile smart` (or `--harness` for explicit control). Route to
harnesses from different model families for maximum coverage.

```bash
# Two-harness adversarial dispatch (parallel)
ddx agent run --harness codex --profile smart \
  --prompt review-target.md \
  > findings-codex.md &

ddx agent run --harness claude --profile smart \
  --prompt review-target.md \
  > findings-claude.md &

wait
```

For three harnesses:

```bash
ddx agent run --harness codex  --profile smart --prompt review-target.md > findings-codex.md  &
ddx agent run --harness claude --profile smart --prompt review-target.md > findings-claude.md &
ddx agent run --harness gemini --profile smart --prompt review-target.md > findings-gemini.md &
wait
```

### 3. Require structured output

Each harness must produce findings in this format. Include this contract
in the prompt:

```markdown
## Output contract

Produce findings as:

### Findings

| Severity | Area | Finding |
|---|---|---|
| BLOCKING | <area> | <specific issue> |
| WARNING  | <area> | <specific issue> |
| NOTE     | <area> | <observation> |

### Verdict: APPROVE | REQUEST_CHANGES | BLOCK

### Summary
<2–4 sentences>
```

A finding with no evidence is invalid. Cite the specific line, section,
or gap that caused the finding. "Looks fine" is not a finding.

### 4. Aggregate

Read all findings files and collate:

1. **BLOCKING** items from any harness → escalate immediately. One BLOCKING
   is enough to stop.
2. **WARNING** items with ≥2 harnesses in agreement → treat as BLOCKING.
3. **WARNING** items unique to one harness → review manually, weigh cost.
4. **NOTE** items → log; address in a follow-up pass or ignore.

Surface disagreements explicitly. If harness A says BLOCKING and harness B
says NOTE for the same area, show both verdicts — do not silently collapse
to the weaker one.

```bash
# Combine findings for review
cat findings-codex.md findings-claude.md > findings-combined.md
```

### 5. Iterate until clean

Revise the target based on BLOCKING findings. Re-run the adversarial review
against the revised version. Typical well-scoped plans converge in 2–3
rounds. Stop when:

- No harness returns a BLOCKING verdict, AND
- All WARNINGs have been explicitly accepted or addressed.

Record the round count and final verdict in the bead evidence:

```bash
ddx bead evidence add <id> --type review --body findings-combined.md
```

## Adversarial framing

The adversarial framing instruction to include in every review prompt:

> You are a critic, not a validator. Your job is to find every way this
> could fail, every constraint it leaves undefined, every assumption it
> bakes in without stating, every interface it leaves ambiguous. A BLOCKING
> finding is anything that would cause implementation rework, a migration
> hazard, or a spec gap that agents will interpret differently. Do not
> balance criticism with praise — a useful adversarial review is entirely
> about what is wrong.

## Personas

Bind a `specification-enforcer` persona for the strictest checks:

```bash
ddx agent run --harness codex \
  --persona specification-enforcer \
  --prompt review-target.md
```

The `specification-enforcer` persona refuses drift from governing artifacts
and surfaces spec gaps others miss.

## Anti-patterns

- **Single-harness adversarial review.** The point is cross-model coverage.
  One harness is bead review, not adversarial review.
- **No structured output contract.** Free-form critique is unaggregateable.
  Always include the findings table + verdict contract in the prompt.
- **Collapsing disagreements.** If harnesses disagree, show both verdicts.
  Hiding disagreement defeats the purpose.
- **Running adversarial review on routine beads.** Reserve for high-stakes
  or controversial work. Routine beads use `ddx bead review`.
- **Stopping after one round.** One round finds problems. Iteration verifies
  that the revision actually resolves them.

## CLI reference

```bash
# Parallel adversarial dispatch
ddx agent run --harness codex  --profile smart --prompt review-target.md > findings-codex.md  &
ddx agent run --harness claude --profile smart --prompt review-target.md > findings-claude.md &
wait

# With persona
ddx agent run --harness codex --persona specification-enforcer \
  --prompt review-target.md > findings-codex.md

# Store findings as evidence
ddx bead evidence add <id> --type review --body findings-combined.md

# Bead review (routine, single-harness — not this skill)
ddx bead review <id>
ddx bead review <id> --execute --harness claude
```
